sources.label: US Regular All Formulations Gas Price
sources.publisher: U.S. Energy Information Administration via FRED

sources.label: U.S. annual retail gasoline prices
sources.publisher: U.S. Energy Information Administration Monthly Energy Review Table 9.4

sources.label: NBER recession indicator
sources.publisher: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis / NBER via FRED

sources.label: Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers
sources.publisher: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics via FRED

eventSources.title: Suez Crisis disrupts oil transit
eventSources.summary: The Suez Crisis is widely identified as a postwar oil shock tied to Middle East conflict and transit disruption.
eventSources.whyIncluded: Adds Cold War-era context before modern weekly gasoline data begins.

eventSources.title: OPEC is founded
eventSources.summary: OPEC was created by Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, and Venezuela to coordinate petroleum policies.
eventSources.whyIncluded: OPEC later became a central institution in global crude oil supply and pricing dynamics.

eventSources.title: Arab oil embargo begins
eventSources.summary: Arab oil producers imposed an embargo and production cuts that sharply increased world oil prices.
eventSources.whyIncluded: One of the defining oil shocks of the Cold War era and a major gasoline-price inflection point.

eventSources.title: Iranian Revolution oil shock
eventSources.summary: Iranian oil output fell sharply during the revolution, contributing to the second major oil shock of the 1970s.
eventSources.whyIncluded: Explains the late-1970s gasoline-price jump visible in annual data.

eventSources.title: Iran-Iraq War begins
eventSources.summary: The Iran-Iraq War followed the Iranian Revolution and is identified in historical oil-shock literature as a major postwar oil-market event.
eventSources.whyIncluded: Adds regional-war context for the early-1980s energy-price environment.

eventSources.title: Iraq invades Kuwait
eventSources.summary: The invasion disrupted Iraqi and Kuwaiti supply and contributed to a rapid crude oil price run-up.
eventSources.whyIncluded: A canonical geopolitical oil shock inside the weekly gasoline price record.

eventSources.title: Oil prices slump during weak demand period
eventSources.summary: Weak petroleum demand and OPEC production choices helped push oil prices sharply lower in the late 1990s.
eventSources.whyIncluded: Shows that gas-price pressure can come from demand weakness, not only wars or supply shocks.

eventSources.title: OPEC production cuts help reverse price collapse
eventSources.summary: A late-1990s oil price shock reversed as OPEC and other producers reduced supply after the 1998 collapse.
eventSources.whyIncluded: A clear example of coordinated producer action affecting oil-market balance.

eventSources.title: Venezuelan oil strike
eventSources.summary: A major Venezuelan strike disrupted oil production and exports, adding pressure to global crude markets.
eventSources.whyIncluded: EIA identifies the strike as one of the major recent crude oil supply disruptions.

eventSources.title: U.S.-led invasion of Iraq
eventSources.summary: Middle East hostilities and supply-risk concerns were part of the early-2000s oil-price environment.
eventSources.whyIncluded: A geopolitically important moment near the beginning of the 2000s oil-price climb.

eventSources.title: Oil price spike before financial crisis collapse
eventSources.summary: Oil prices reached extreme highs in 2008 before the global financial crisis drove demand and prices downward.
eventSources.whyIncluded: A major modern example of gasoline prices, crude oil, and macro stress moving together.

eventSources.title: Libya conflict and Arab Spring supply risk
eventSources.summary: Political instability in oil-producing regions raised supply-risk concerns during a period of elevated crude prices.
eventSources.whyIncluded: A post-2008 geopolitical risk period visible in oil and gasoline markets.

eventSources.title: OPEC maintains output amid oil-price collapse
eventSources.summary: Global crude markets shifted into oversupply, contributing to a steep oil-price decline from 2014 into 2016.
eventSources.whyIncluded: A major OPEC-era supply decision that coincides with a clear gasoline-price downshift.

eventSources.title: Attack on Saudi Aramco Abqaiq and Khurais facilities
eventSources.summary: Attacks disrupted Saudi crude production and showed how disruption risk can move prices rapidly.
eventSources.whyIncluded: EIA specifically analyzes the immediate price effect of this supply-disruption risk.

eventSources.title: COVID-19 emergency and gasoline demand collapse
eventSources.summary: Pandemic responses sharply reduced travel and gasoline demand, pushing U.S. gasoline prices to multi-year lows.
eventSources.whyIncluded: A demand-side shock that strongly affected both fuel prices and equity markets.

eventSources.title: Russia further invades Ukraine
eventSources.summary: Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine and sanctions risk pushed crude oil above $100 per barrel and raised gasoline-price expectations.
eventSources.whyIncluded: One of the clearest recent geopolitical shocks in both gasoline and market data.

eventSources.title: Crude prices peak then ease as supply and demand conditions shift
eventSources.summary: Crude prices generally fell after early June 2022 as recession concerns, China demand weakness, and strategic reserve releases affected supply-demand balance.
eventSources.whyIncluded: Shows that policy, demand, and inventories can matter alongside war-related risk.

eventSources.title: Strait of Hormuz chokepoint context
eventSources.summary: EIA identifies the Strait of Hormuz as the world's most important oil transit chokepoint, with roughly one-fifth of global petroleum liquids consumption moving through it in recent years.
eventSources.whyIncluded: Adds trusted context for interpreting Persian Gulf blockade or shipping-risk scenarios without claiming an unsourced closure event.
